title: The Promotion

jokes.text: No way, no how. Expense reports are a day-to-day item. That is Jim's now. I am exclusively big picture. Epic.
jokes.setup: Employee asks Michael to sign routine expense report
jokes.punchline: Michael declares himself 'exclusively big picture. Epic' to avoid basic managerial tasks
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael Scott character moment - grandiose self-importance to avoid mundane work, with the absurd 'Epic' tag

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: Michael, could you sign off on this expense report?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I'm exclusively big picture. Epic. I don't do the small stuff anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Employee
dialogue.line: It's literally your job to approve these.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's middle management thinking. I'm visionary now.

jokes.text: Where's the please? We're not animals.
jokes.setup: Jim asks Michael to sign something without saying please
jokes.punchline: Michael demands politeness while being petty and obstructive
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Michael's hypocritical demand for respect while being childish

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, can you sign this?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Did you say the magic word?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Please?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I don't know, Jim. I'm gonna need you to be a little more sincere about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, please sign the document.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Hmm, still not feeling it. Maybe try again tomorrow.

jokes.text: I have a complaint about Jim. That is not big picture. I would like to file a huge, enormous, massive complaint about the tallest guy in our office.
jokes.setup: Dwight tries to complain about Jim, Michael dismisses as not big picture
jokes.punchline: Dwight escalates with 'huge, enormous, massive' and refers to Jim as 'tallest guy' to make it seem bigger
jokes.explanation: Dwight's typical over-the-top escalation and literal-minded attempt to make complaint sound 'big picture'

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim is a huge problem. Enormous. Massive.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, that's not big picture thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The tallest guy in the office is ruining everything!

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, now that's big picture.

jokes.text: Get out, get out, get out, get out, get out.
jokes.setup: Michael realizes Dwight is trying to trick him into handling day-to-day issues
jokes.punchline: Michael's childish repetitive dismissal when caught in his own logic trap
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective character moment showing Michael's immaturity when cornered

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not falling for it, Dwight. I know what you're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't know what you mean, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yes, you do. You're trying to get me to handle all the office stuff so you can slack off.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's not true. But someone has to do these things.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Not me. Not happening. I'm the boss. I don't do that stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: So who will do it?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You will.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: But you just said I'm trying to trick you into doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know what I said. And I'm not doing it. I'm not, I'm not, I'm not doing it.

jokes.text: I'd like to lodge a complaint. I'm sorry to hear about that. Who is this about? You.
jokes.setup: Michael asks who the complaint is about in professional manner
jokes.punchline: Jim reveals the complaint is about Michael himself
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline structure with good timing and ironic reversal

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Who is the complaint about?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's about you.

jokes.text: I'm just going to write, 'Held back tears.' Wait, stop writing that. That is not true. If you stop crying, I'll stop writing it. I'm not...
jokes.setup: Michael takes Jim's complaint seriously and starts documenting
jokes.punchline: Michael gaslights Jim about crying while Jim protests he's not crying
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael move - creating false narrative while Jim gets increasingly frustrated

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, I'm documenting everything you say. For the record.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, I'm not crying.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Your eyes are red, Jim. I can see the tears.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm not crying! I'm just frustrated.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's okay to cry, Jim. I'm writing down that you cried. For documentation purposes.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, stop. I didn't cry.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what you said last time, right before you cried.

jokes.text: Oh, man, makes me want to put him in a triangle choke hold and force him down to the ground and just keep pressing and pressing, and then flip him over and then put him in a hammerlock and he's gasping. He's panting for every last breath. And the crowd is going crazy and boom, I emerge victorious! Eighteen thousand dollars and a chance at the title!
jokes.setup: Dwight expresses anger about Jim getting the promotion
jokes.punchline: Escalates into elaborate wrestling fantasy with specific prize money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight escalation - starts with workplace frustration, becomes elaborate wrestling fantasy with specific details

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim got the promotion? That should have been me! I am clearly the most qualified person in this office.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: You know what? I challenge Jim to a wrestling match. Winner takes all. Loser has to leave Dunder Mifflin forever.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The prize pool? $50,000. No, $100,000. I'm putting my own money in because I'm that confident.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'll body slam him so hard they'll feel it in Scranton for years. I've been training in Schrute Farms fighting techniques since I was three years old.

jokes.text: Dwight, Jim wants you to keep it down.
jokes.setup: Dwight just finished loud wrestling fantasy about defeating Jim
jokes.punchline: Pam delivers message from Jim asking Dwight to be quieter
jokes.explanation: Good timing and irony - Jim's indirect response to Dwight's violent fantasy about him

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Jim wanted me to tell you that he can hear everything you're saying, and he thinks it's weird.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: FALSE. Impossible. My office is soundproof.

jokes.text: Can I also be a boss?
jokes.setup: Jan asks to speak with Michael in her office
jokes.punchline: Michael's childish request to also be a boss, missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Captures Michael's childlike understanding of authority and competition

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: Michael, I need to speak with you in my office.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay. Am I in trouble?

dialogue.speaker: Jan
dialogue.line: No, I just need to discuss some things about your management style.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, can I also be a boss? Because I feel like I should get to be a boss too.

jokes.text: Go ahead, name a country that doesn't have two presidents. A boat that set sail without two captains. Where would Catholicism be without the popes?
jokes.setup: Michael argues that organizations thrive with two leaders
jokes.punchline: Gives completely wrong examples - no country has two presidents, boats don't have two captains, Catholicism has one pope
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Michael logic - confident delivery of completely incorrect examples that actually disprove his point

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Organizations thrive when they have two leaders. It's a proven fact.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Look at countries — they all have two presidents.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And boats? Two captains. It's the only way they stay afloat.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Even the Catholic Church — two popes.

jokes.text: Jimothy. To be fair, Jimothy... That sounds weird. Are you okay with being called Jim? I am.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to use formal version of Jim's name
jokes.punchline: Invents non-existent name 'Jimothy', immediately realizes it sounds wrong
jokes.explanation: Became iconic moment - Michael's confident invention of fake formal name, immediate self-correction

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: James, I'd like to speak with you about—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's just Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Right, right. Jimothy, let me explain something.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Jimothy?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not a real name, is it?

jokes.text: At first, we were talking about introducing a line of toilet paper. And what part of the human body does one use toilet paper upon? So you draw a line from there to the other planets, and I think by the end, we all learned a little bit about how small we are.
jokes.setup: Michael defends yesterday's meeting about planets
jokes.punchline: Reveals bizarre logical connection from toilet paper to butts to planets to existential smallness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael pseudo-intellectual rambling with hilariously tenuous logical connections

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Look, yesterday's meeting about planets was not a waste of time. I was making a very important point about perspective. You see, we use toilet paper on our butts, right? And butts are small. Planets are huge. But we're even smaller than the planets. So really, when you think about it, we're all just... we're all just toilet paper for the universe.

jokes.text: You know what eats a large amount of the day are naps. You go to sleep, it's light out, you wake up, it's dark. That's the whole day. Where did that day go? I have no idea.
jokes.setup: Jim mentions meetings eating up the day
jokes.punchline: Michael relates it to naps with genuine confusion about how time works
jokes.explanation: Relatable observation delivered with Michael's characteristic obliviousness to his own work avoidance

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know what you mean. I had a nap yesterday at like 2 o'clock and I woke up and it was 2:30. Where did that half hour go?

jokes.text: Really? You just agree to that? I can be very surprising.
jokes.setup: Jim is shocked Michael agrees to no more meetings
jokes.punchline: Michael's proud declaration of being 'surprising' for doing something reasonable
jokes.explanation: Michael's misplaced pride in basic reasonableness he sees as unexpected

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, you're actually agreeing to this?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm surprising.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not... that's not what surprising means.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, I'm surprising. I do unexpected things all the time.

jokes.text: I need to fit into my wedding dress. However, I'm also pregnant.
jokes.setup: Pam states she needs to fit into her wedding dress
jokes.punchline: Reveals the obvious contradiction - she's pregnant
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective setup/punchline about the practical challenges of pregnant wedding planning

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I need to fit into my wedding dress.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I'm pregnant.

jokes.text: You know, it's a myth that women have to gain more than nine pounds in a pregnancy. Look at these actresses. Some of them lose weight.
jokes.setup: Pam dealing with pregnancy weight and wedding dress
jokes.punchline: Cites unrealistic celebrity pregnancy standards as if they're achievable
jokes.explanation: Relatable commentary on unrealistic pregnancy expectations with Pam's characteristic self-deception

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I don't know why I'm worried. I mean, look at all these celebrities who lose their baby weight in like two weeks. Kim Kardashian, Beyoncé... I can do that.

jokes.text: Why doesn't Crate and Barrel let you register for a toaster full of cash?
jokes.setup: Pam and Jim want cash gifts instead of registry items
jokes.punchline: Imagines impractical hybrid of traditional gift with cash desire
jokes.explanation: Creative visual image combining wedding registry tradition with modern cash preference

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We were thinking we'd really like cash.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, just cash.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But we also want to be thoughtful about it, so maybe people could give us cash, but in a really nice box.

jokes.text: My cousin makes the most amazing romantic birdhouse mailboxes. I know I shouldn't tell you, but you'll still be surprised when you see it. You're not registered for a birdhouse, are you? No, we're not. Good. Good.
jokes.setup: Phyllis mentions her cousin makes birdhouse mailboxes as gifts
jokes.punchline: Awkward exchange where Phyllis confirms they're getting something they don't want
jokes.explanation: Typical Phyllis moment - well-meaning but completely missing social cues about gift appropriateness

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: My cousin makes birdhouse mailboxes. He's giving us one as a gift.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh, that's... nice.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're really excited about it.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Phyllis
dialogue.line: Well, actually, we don't really want it, but we have to take it because he's family.

jokes.text: If you don't smell this, you're fired.
jokes.setup: Stanley reports what Michael said before Jim entered
jokes.punchline: Michael was threatening to fire people for not smelling something
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Michael threat - making smelling something a job requirement

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: Michael was in here threatening to fire people for not smelling something.

jokes.text: Oh, hey, everybody, I think that Jim has gone insane because he thinks that my office is a conference room.
jokes.setup: Jim tries to end Michael's impromptu meeting
jokes.punchline: Michael gaslights everyone about the obvious meeting happening in conference room
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael deflection - accusing Jim of insanity to avoid admitting he's breaking their agreement

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, weren't you just in a meeting in the conference room?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What? No. I've been here the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: But we all saw you go in there like five minutes ago.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous. You're all being crazy. There is no meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I was in the meeting with you, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Dwight, stop it. You're confusing everyone. This is exactly why we need to focus on the work in front of us instead of making up stories.

jokes.text: Anything that Jim has to say to me, he can say in front of these types of people.
jokes.setup: Jim asks for private moment to discuss meeting issue
jokes.punchline: Michael refers to employees as 'these types of people' while claiming transparency
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual dehumanizing language while trying to appear inclusive and transparent

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, can we have a private moment? There's a meeting issue I wanted to discuss.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Of course, Jim. You know I'm all about transparency and open communication with these types of people.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: ...these types of people?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Employees. I mean, the staff. You know what I mean.

jokes.text: I love rivalries. Michael or Jim, Paris or Nicole, Heidi or L.C., it's so much fun. But I guess if I'm really thinking about it, and answering your question honestly, I'd have to go with L.C. Heidi's a bad friend and her skin is terrible.
jokes.setup: Kelly comments on Michael vs Jim rivalry
jokes.punchline: Completely ignores office situation to give detailed opinion about reality TV rivalry
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly moment - takes workplace conflict and makes it about celebrity drama with specific harsh opinions

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you guys are like so obsessed with each other. It's like when Heidi and Spencer were fighting on The Hills. But honestly, Heidi was totally justified because Spencer was being a complete snake, and everyone knows that LC should have stuck with Stephen instead of going back to Jason, and don't even get me started on what happened at the Heidi and Spencer wedding because that was literally the most dramatic moment in reality TV history.

jokes.text: It is one thing me giving you the co-manager title, it is another thing you bossing me around.
jokes.setup: Michael and Jim discuss their co-manager arrangement
jokes.punchline: Michael claims he 'gave' Jim the title when Wallace actually promoted Jim
jokes.explanation: Michael's revision of history to maintain sense of authority and control

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I gave you the co-manager title.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, you didn't. Wallace promoted me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I let him.

jokes.text: This is Michael, senior co-manager.
jokes.setup: Michael answers phone call from corporate
jokes.punchline: Invents 'senior co-manager' title to assert dominance over Jim
jokes.explanation: Michael's immediate attempt to establish hierarchy with made-up title during important call

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Hello? Yes, this is Michael Scott speaking.

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Representative
dialogue.line: Michael, we're calling about the branch performance metrics.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate you calling, but I should tell you that I'm not just the Regional Manager anymore. I'm also the Senior Co-Manager.

dialogue.speaker: Corporate Representative
dialogue.line: Senior Co-Manager? That's not a position we have.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, you do now. It's a brand new position that I created. Very prestigious. It means I have authority over all the managers, including Jim.

jokes.text: Wait, I'm sorry. Michael, are you texting me?
jokes.setup: David Wallace is on important conference call about raises
jokes.punchline: Michael was texting David during the call instead of participating professionally
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - Michael's inappropriate behavior during serious corporate call

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: So as I was saying, we need to discuss the quarterly budget allocations and—

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: That's what she said.

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: Michael, are you even paying attention?

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: Of course I am. You're talking about... raises?

dialogue.speaker: David Wallace
dialogue.line: I can see you texting under the table.

dialogue.speaker: Michael Scott
dialogue.line: I'm not texting. I'm taking notes.

jokes.text: Okay, you didn't let me say goodbye.
jokes.setup: Important corporate call ends
jokes.punchline: Michael's petty complaint about phone etiquette after his texting interruption
jokes.explanation: Michael's misplaced priorities - focusing on goodbye etiquette after major disruption

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what? I find it very rude when people text during a phone call. It's disrespectful.

jokes.text: When I am irritated and I look at you, I don't see you anymore. Right. All I can see is how big and gross the pores on your nose are.
jokes.setup: Michael explains his working relationship issues with Jim
jokes.punchline: Gets weirdly specific about Jim's facial pores as distraction
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortably personal and specific insult that reveals more about Michael's pettiness than Jim's appearance

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know, Jim and I have our ups and downs, but at the end of the day, we're a team.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Although, I have to say, lately I've been noticing his pores are getting really large. Like, disturbingly large. Has anyone else noticed that? They're like... little craters on his face.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm not trying to be mean, but it's hard to focus on anything he's saying when I'm just staring at those pores. It's like the surface of the moon up there.

jokes.text: Hey, why haven't we ever... We have.
jokes.setup: Jim starts to ask Pam something personal
jokes.punchline: Pam immediately shuts down the question, implying they've already discussed/done whatever he was asking
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy - entire relationship dynamic in four words, with Pam's quick defensive response

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Pam, can I ask you something personal?

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We've already done it.

jokes.text: Do you mean, like, break in, in the middle of the night and change the numbers on payroll? No, we can do it during the day. It doesn't have to be that dramatic, Jim.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests adding 1.5% to paychecks without telling anyone
jokes.punchline: Jim assumes this requires breaking in at night, Michael casually says they can do it during normal hours
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Jim's assumption of criminal activity and Michael's casual acceptance of workplace fraud

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we're breaking in at night?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We can just do it during normal business hours.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: During business hours? Michael, that's insane.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Why? I'm the regional manager. I can do whatever I want.

jokes.text: You use your brain too much. I'm sorry, are you advocating that I use it less? Sometimes the smartest people don't think at all. You just came up with that. As I was saying it. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Michael criticizes Jim for overthinking
jokes.punchline: Michael admits he's making up philosophy on the spot while claiming smart people don't think
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-defeating logic from Michael - admits to not thinking while criticizing thinking, perfectly delivered

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is, Jim? You think too much. Smart people, they don't think. They just... know things.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. See, you're thinking about it again.

jokes.text: Con, you look like a nerd. Pro, you get to share your pros and cons list with the other nerds. Con, you unzip your pants and you'll find that there's a calculator down there.
jokes.setup: Michael mockingly adds to Jim's pros and cons list
jokes.punchline: Escalates to crude joke about Jim having calculator instead of genitals
jokes.explanation: Michael's escalating mockery gets progressively more juvenile, ending in crude but somewhat clever nerd stereotype

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, Jim, Jim. Let me add to your pros and cons list. Pro: you're tall. Con: you have a calculator for genitals.

jokes.text: I've been studying Michael for years and I've condensed what I've learned into this chart. How Michael spends his time. As you can see, we have procrastinating and distracting others. And this tiny sliver here is critical thinking. I made it bigger so that you could see it.
jokes.setup: Jim explains his scientific study of Michael's behavior
jokes.punchline: Chart shows Michael does almost no critical thinking, with tiny slice made bigger for visibility
jokes.explanation: Brilliant visual comedy concept - Jim's scientific approach to Michael's incompetence, with self-aware joke about tiny slice

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've been conducting a scientific study of Michael's behavior. As you can see from this chart, Michael does almost no critical thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: This tiny slice had to be made bigger for visibility.

jokes.text: I don't think we registered anywhere. We just want cash. Like money? Like, you want my money? Mmm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Pam explains they want cash instead of gifts
jokes.punchline: Creed's blunt interpretation that they literally want his money, Pam's unashamed confirmation
jokes.explanation: Creed cuts through wedding gift politeness to expose the basic transaction, Pam's honest agreement

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: We don't want any gifts. We'd really just prefer cash.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: So you want my money.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: In the memo line, I'm going to write, 'To love's eternal glory.'
jokes.setup: Creed agrees to write a check for wedding gift
jokes.punchline: Plans elaborate romantic memo for what's essentially a cash transaction
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed - unexpectedly poetic flourish on mundane transaction, fits his mysterious character

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: I'll write a check for the wedding gift.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Let me make this memo really romantic and elaborate. 'For the happy couple, may your love be as timeless as the memories we share, as deep as the ocean, as boundless as the sky...'

jokes.text: Is this what I've become? Materialistic? Shallow? I feel horrible. Oh, look! 'Mrs. Pam Halpert!' That's the first time I've seen it in writing.
jokes.setup: Pam has moral crisis about asking for money
jokes.punchline: Immediately distracted by seeing her future married name in writing
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Pam's self-awareness immediately trumped by romantic excitement

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: I can't ask for money. What if they say no? I'll be humiliated. But what if they say yes? Then I'll owe them forever and they'll use it against me in every argument we ever have.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Wait... Pam Halpert. Pam... Halpert. Oh my God, that's going to be my name.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, are you going to make another pro and con list? I'm going to kill myself.
jokes.setup: Jim suggests making another pros and cons list
jokes.punchline: Michael's dramatic threat of suicide over list-making
jokes.explanation: Michael's typical over-dramatic reaction to Jim's methodical approach

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We should make another pros and cons list.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, if you make me do another pros and cons list, I will kill myself.

jokes.text: I can see it in your crusty little eyes that you are going to make everything perfect. 'If I could just think this through, if I could just think it exactly right, I can make this perfect. And then I can go down every avenue, and every avenue off of that avenue. And then another avenue...' And then another.
jokes.setup: Michael mocks Jim's analytical approach
jokes.punchline: Escalating impression of Jim's overthinking with infinite avenue metaphor
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Michael's impression captures Jim's analytical nature while revealing his own impatience

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, Jim's so analytical. 'Well, let me think about this. There's avenue one, and avenue two, and avenue three...' And then what, Jim? Avenue four? Avenue five? You're just gonna keep going down avenues forever?

jokes.text: I think that you are able to take constructive criticism very well. Ha! That, I am not known for that.
jokes.setup: Jim tells Michael his strengths include taking criticism well
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately proves the opposite by rejecting the compliment
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic moment - Michael demonstrates his inability to accept criticism while being told he's good at it

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Your strengths include taking criticism well.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's not true. I don't take criticism well at all.

jokes.text: Okay. Here's a tough decision for you, you suck. You suck. Is that clear enough for you?
jokes.setup: Jim says Michael isn't good at making tough decisions clearly
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'tough decision' is just insulting Jim twice
jokes.explanation: Michael completely misunderstands the criticism and responds with childish insults instead of decisions

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, fine. You're an idiot, Jim. And you're also a jerk.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not a tough decision, that's just insulting me twice.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, it was a tough decision. I had to choose which insult to use first.

jokes.text: Okay, Skippy, here we go. Why don't you run yourself out there and tell them? You call it.
jokes.setup: Michael decides to let Jim handle the difficult announcement
jokes.punchline: Condescendingly calls Jim 'Skippy' while dumping responsibility on him
jokes.explanation: Michael's passive-aggressive way of avoiding difficult management tasks while maintaining superiority

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Alright Skippy, why don't you go ahead and tell everyone the bad news?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know what your name is, Skippy.

jokes.text: This had better be terrible.
jokes.setup: Michael watches Jim prepare to deliver bad news to staff
jokes.punchline: Michael hopes Jim fails rather than wanting good outcome for office
jokes.explanation: Reveals Michael's petty desire to see Jim fail rather than caring about employee wellbeing

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I hope he fails. I really do. I hope he crashes and burns, and I'm sitting right here in the front row, watching it happen.

jokes.text: But I do not understand. If we're such smart adults, then why don't we get raises?
jokes.setup: Jim compliments staff intelligence while denying them raises
jokes.punchline: Oscar points out the logical contradiction
jokes.explanation: Oscar's logical mind exposes the condescending nature of Jim's explanation

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You're all incredibly intelligent. I value your minds.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: Then why won't you pay us more?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Because... those are two separate things.

dialogue.speaker: Oscar
dialogue.line: No, they're not. If you value something, you pay for it. I value my car, so I pay for it. I value my apartment, so I pay for it. By your logic, you don't actually value us at all.

jokes.text: Excellent question, genius. It's because Jim wants to give the raises to his friends and the people he sleeps with.
jokes.setup: Oscar asks logical question about raise distribution
jokes.punchline: Michael sarcastically calls him genius then makes inappropriate accusation about Jim
jokes.explanation: Michael undermines Jim with inappropriate insinuation while calling Oscar genius sarcastically

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh, you're a genius. You're a genius! Why didn't I think of that? Oh, that's right, because I'm not an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, is Jim paying you to say this? Is that what's happening here?

jokes.text: How is that going to repair Ryan's car?
jokes.setup: Jim says knowing Kelly is smart should help her feel better
jokes.punchline: Kelly immediately makes it about Ryan and material needs
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kelly moment - ignores compliment to focus on Ryan's material needs

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Knowing that you're smart should help you feel better.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Well, it would help me feel better if Ryan knew I was smart. And if I had a yacht. Or like, a jet ski.

jokes.text: My kid needs shoes. You want to tell him he doesn't get shoes? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Meredith uses her child to guilt trip about raises
jokes.punchline: Michael casually agrees he'd tell her kid he doesn't get shoes
jokes.explanation: Michael's shocking lack of empathy delivered with casual cruelty

dialogue.speaker: Meredith
dialogue.line: My son needs new shoes. I can't afford them on what you pay me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, well, I'll tell him he doesn't get shoes.

jokes.text: They all have heart-ons for you.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to say the staff care about Jim
jokes.punchline: Invents portmanteau of 'heart' and inappropriate word
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael inappropriate wordplay - trying to be touching but making it sexual

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: The staff really does care about Jim. We have a lot of... heart. A lot of... hass.

jokes.text: My plan, a man, Panama. That's not how that goes.
jokes.setup: Staff asks for Michael's plan for raises
jokes.punchline: Michael attempts palindrome but gets it wrong, Jim corrects him
jokes.explanation: Michael tries to be clever with wordplay but fails at basic palindrome structure

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A man, a plan, a canal... Panama!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's not a palindrome, Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: What? Yes it is.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, it's not. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's exactly what I said.

jokes.text: All right, everyone wants a raise, so what we're going to do is go into here and we will not come out until we do.
jokes.setup: Michael promises to solve the raise problem
jokes.punchline: Vague plan to lock themselves in room until unspecified resolution
jokes.explanation: Michael's non-solution presented as decisive action, typical avoidance behavior

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, here's what we're gonna do. We're gonna lock ourselves in the conference room until corporate agrees to give us raises.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: That's a hostage situation.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, it's not a hostage situation. We're hostages... to our own ambition.

jokes.text: Ryan is obviously the best. Obviously. Too bad he's a temp and doesn't count, so let's get started.
jokes.setup: Michael ranking employees by merit
jokes.punchline: Declares Ryan best then immediately disqualifies him for being temp
jokes.explanation: Michael's favoritism toward Ryan exposed then immediately made irrelevant

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm ranking everyone by merit. Ryan, you're number one.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Wait, you're a temp. You're disqualified.

jokes.text: Each Boston baked bean represents half-a-percent raise. We each got 24 beans, so the idea is, you place the bean on the picture of who you think deserves...
jokes.setup: Michael explains his bean-based raise distribution system
jokes.punchline: Elaborate system using beans on photos to determine employee worth
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd visual gag - reducing employee worth to literal beans on photos

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I have developed a revolutionary system for determining raises. I take a photo of each employee, print it out, and then place beans on their photo based on their performance metrics.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: More beans means more money. It's foolproof. I call it the Bean-Based Compensation Matrix.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: For example, Jim gets seven beans because he closes deals. Pam gets five beans because she's... Pam. And Dwight gets the whole bag because he's Dwight.

jokes.text: Who's that? Toby. Okay, he's not a part of this, you know that. Just wanted to draw a picture of him.
jokes.setup: Jim notices extra photo in bean game
jokes.punchline: Michael included Toby just to draw his picture despite excluding him
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Michael's Toby obsession - includes him just to exclude him

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Why is there a picture of Toby in here?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I needed to draw his picture.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: But I thought Toby wasn't allowed to play.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: He's not. But I had to include him so I could exclude him from the game after I drew his picture.

jokes.text: I will skip a turn. Okay, you're going to still have to play that bean, you know that. I need more time.
jokes.setup: Michael can't decide who deserves raise beans
jokes.punchline: Tries to skip turn in his own game, needs more time for simple decision
jokes.explanation: Michael's paralysis when faced with actual decision-making in his own system

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I need to think about this. This is a big decision.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, it's just a raise. Pick someone.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You don't understand. This is my game. My rules. And I... I need more time.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: You literally made up the rules five minutes ago.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. Which is why I should be allowed to skip my turn. I'm the boss.

jokes.text: If the people here were our Founding Fathers, the Revolutionary War would have been delayed 10 years because Stanley Washington was napping. And Phyllis Hancock was still signing the Declaration, and Kevin Jefferson was distracted by a butterfly.
jokes.setup: Dwight complains about coworkers' slow response to Jim's poor management
jokes.punchline: Elaborate historical analogy with each coworker as Founding Father with characteristic flaws
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Dwight's grandiose historical metaphor perfectly captures each coworker's personality

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim's management style is equivalent to the Continental Congress. Ineffective, slow to respond, and populated by men who should never have been given power.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Kevin is Benjamin Franklin - he shows up occasionally, contributes nothing of value, and smells like he's been in a tavern.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley is Thomas Jefferson - he claims to have principles, but he's really just lazy and contradicts himself.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Pam is John Adams - undersized, shrill, and utterly ineffectual despite thinking she's indispensable.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: And Ryan is Benedict Arnold. Young, ambitious, and completely untrustworthy.

jokes.text: Why aren't there any beans on this very old, frizzy-haired picture of me?
jokes.setup: Creed notices he has no beans on his photo
jokes.punchline: Describes his own photo as 'very old, frizzy-haired picture'
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed self-awareness about his appearance while missing the point about merit

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: This is a very old, frizzy-haired picture of me.

jokes.text: Not according to the beans.
jokes.setup: Kevin claims his time is as valuable as Jim's
jokes.punchline: Jim deadpan refers to bean distribution as authority
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery - treating absurd bean system as legitimate authority with complete economy

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: My time is just as valuable as yours, Jim.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Kevin, you spend half your day microwaving beans in the break room.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm in charge of bean distribution. That's a position of authority.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I report directly to corporate. So really, you report to me through the bean hierarchy.

jokes.text: Is he as matronly as Phyllis?
jokes.setup: Dwight questions Jim's qualifications for authority
jokes.punchline: Describes Phyllis as 'matronly' as if it's a professional qualification
jokes.explanation: Dwight's weird logic treating Phyllis's matronly nature as workplace asset

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Jim, you don't have the qualifications to be in a position of authority. You lack discipline, focus, and any real business acumen.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, and what qualifications do you have, Dwight?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have studied under Halpert's mistakes, I have mastered the art of the sale, and I have cultivated a network of alliances that would make a senator weep. Unlike you, I have real credentials.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What about Phyllis?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Phyllis is matronly. That's a professional qualification.

jokes.text: Let us storm his castle. Come on, tick, let's get him, tock. Let's get Jim. Tick, and drag Jim out of his office, tock, take his keys away from him, tick, that's a clock.
jokes.setup: Dwight rallies coworkers against Jim
jokes.punchline: Medieval revolution speech interrupted by clock sound effects and explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight moment - grandiose medieval rhetoric undercut by literal clock sounds and explanation

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: The time has come for us to rise up against the tyranny of Jim Halpert! He sits at his desk, mocking us with his smirks and his non-threatening behavior. But no more! We will storm his cubicle like peasants storming a castle!

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: *medieval horn sound* Wait, what was that?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Oh, it's just the clock. We installed medieval sound effects on the office clock system. It goes off every hour.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Anyway, as I was saying, we must— *bell tolling sound* —dammit!

jokes.text: No, I mean, what do you say to my plan?
jokes.setup: Stanley says no to Dwight's revolution
jokes.punchline: Dwight clarifies he wants opinion on plan, not participation
jokes.explanation: Dwight's failed attempt to save face after revolution gets no support

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley, I need your opinion on something. I'm starting a revolution against Michael's management style.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I haven't even told you the plan yet.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: I don't care. No.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley, I'm not asking you to participate. I just want your opinion.

jokes.text: What does a bean mean?
jokes.setup: Kevin continues to be confused by bean system
jokes.punchline: Still doesn't understand basic concept after multiple explanations
jokes.explanation: Kevin's persistent confusion becomes running gag, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: Wait, so the beans... they're not real beans?

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: But I've been eating them this whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: So what have I been putting in my chili?

jokes.text: I used to have to do this part alone. And it was the worst.
jokes.setup: Michael reflects on his management experience with Jim
jokes.punchline: Admits the 'worst' part of management was making actual decisions
jokes.explanation: Rare moment of Michael honesty about his inadequacy as manager

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You know what the worst part about management is, Jim? It's not the long hours, it's not the paperwork, it's not even dealing with corporate. It's having to make actual decisions.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, that's literally the main part of your job.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. That's why it's the worst.

jokes.text: What's in here? Gin.
jokes.setup: Michael gives Jim a wrapped gift
jokes.punchline: Casually reveals it's alcohol during work crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and timing - Michael's inappropriate workplace gift delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Jim, I got you something.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, thanks Michael.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It's a bottle of scotch. For emergencies.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Michael, it's 9 AM on a Tuesday.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Exactly. You never know when you'll need it.

jokes.text: Question for you, would you guys rather have $100 now or $5,000 a year from now? $100 now, for sure. Because you just give me $50 to cover the broker fee, I put in $100 of my own money, as the gift.
jokes.setup: Creed offers wedding gift choice between amounts
jokes.punchline: Reveals elaborate scam requiring them to pay him for the privilege
jokes.explanation: Perfect Creed scam - presents as generous gift option but requires them to pay him

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: So for your wedding gift, I can either give you $500 or $1000.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: Oh wow, Creed, that's so generous!

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Yeah, but here's the thing. If you pick the $1000, you have to give me $2000 first.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: That's... that's not how gifts work.

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: It's a reverse funnel system. You're actually making money.

jokes.text: How sure is this? The guy has an algorithm to determine the winner of any given college basketball game.
jokes.setup: Jim asks about certainty of Creed's investment scheme
jokes.punchline: Creed's confidence based on sports gambling algorithm
jokes.explanation: Classic Creed - presents obviously unreliable gambling system as foolproof investment

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So you're confident about this investment?

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: Absolutely. I've got a sports gambling algorithm that's 87% accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: How is that relevant to this investment?

dialogue.speaker: Creed
dialogue.line: It's not. But I'm very confident in it.

tmdb_overview: David Wallace comes bearing the news that not all the staffers will receive a raise this year. Jim pushes Dwight to the limit and causes him to form office alliances. And Pam decides she wants only cash as a wedding gift.
insight: Cringe-heavy episode underperforms: 73 humor score despite 63 jokes in 42 minutes.
